summary: Reviewed the planned 60-second timing change and host dashboard table conversion against the architecture shown in the pack. The main risk is that reveal timing is treated as both session-owned configuration and duplicated UI literals, which can make legacy 40s sessions display incorrect timing even if playback logic still honors stored config. No implemented diff was present, so findings are spec-level or based on current source evidence.
appears_sound: Reveal phase computation is centralized in `lib/live/reveal.ts:10` via `getRevealPhase(progressMs, cfg = DEFAULT_REVEAL_CONFIG)`, so timing behavior can be controlled through a `RevealConfig` contract.
Session timing is explicitly modeled as persisted session data in `LiveSessionV1.revealConfig` at `lib/live/types.ts:54`, which supports legacy sessions retaining their stored timings.

findings.description: The spec says existing sessions with stored 40s config keep working, but the guest display's normal-song countdown is planned as a hardcoded 60s baseline instead of reading `session.revealConfig.nextMs`. Legacy 40s sessions would likely show `Next song at 60s` while the host/runtime can advance at 40s, creating cross-device UI drift.
findings.evidence: Current guest code computes `Next song at` from `(runtime.isChallengeSong ? 90_000 : 30_000) + runtime.extensionMs` at `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:354`, while session-owned `revealConfig` exists at `lib/live/types.ts:54`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The host may already normalize legacy sessions to the new config before runtime updates, but that would contradict the spec's stated no-migration behavior.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check the host runtime update path to verify whether normal-song reveal thresholds come from `session.revealConfig` for persisted sessions and update guest display to use the same source.

findings.description: The spec preserves duplicated reveal timing literals in client UI copy instead of deriving labels from the shared reveal config. This conflicts with the stated architecture that `DEFAULT_REVEAL_CONFIG` controls timing and makes future timing changes require another multi-file hunt.
findings.evidence: Guest placeholders currently hardcode `Album reveals at 10s`, `Title reveals at 20s`, and `Artist reveals at 25s` at `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:317`, `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:330`, and `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:340`, while reveal logic is configurable in `lib/live/reveal.ts:10`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The UI copy may intentionally describe default timing only, but the page already loads the session object and can access the actual session timing.
findings.what_would_confirm: Verify whether guest UI should reflect per-session timing; if yes, derive these strings from `session.revealConfig` or a shared timing-label helper.

findings.description: Host controller badge labels and fallback timing values are listed as direct literal replacements, but the pack does not show the surrounding host runtime code that owns reveal-state computation. This leaves unclear whether host controls, runtime state, and display labels all consume the same effective config for default, challenge, extension, and legacy-session paths.
findings.evidence: The spec calls for changing hardcoded host labels and fallback `nextMs` values at `app/host/[sessionId]/page.tsx:1025`, `app/host/[sessionId]/page.tsx:1052`, and `app/host/[sessionId]/page.tsx:1057`, but the related host controller source is not included in the pack.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Those host literals may be purely presentational fallbacks and the actual runtime may already use `session.revealConfig` correctly.
findings.what_would_confirm: Review the omitted host controller timing path to ensure all normal-song reveal and auto-advance decisions use a single effective `RevealConfig`, with challenge overrides applied deliberately.

findings.description: The table conversion removes the explicit `changingBrand` toggle, but the spec does not say whether the now-unused `changingBrand` state will be removed or whether brand update behavior will be centralized for inline selectors. Leaving stale state or duplicating inline mutation handlers per row would make ownership of dashboard row mutations less clear.
findings.evidence: Current dashboard owns brand-edit visibility in `changingBrand` at `app/host/page.tsx:21` and performs the brand update inline inside each card's `BrandSelector` at `app/host/page.tsx:330`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The implementation may naturally delete `changingBrand` and keep a single inline handler when replacing the cards with a table.
findings.what_would_confirm: Inspect the final `app/host/page.tsx` diff to verify stale brand-toggle state is removed and the inline table selector still calls the same refresh/error/notice flow.

empty_categories: No schema migration risk found in the pack; `revealConfig` is stored inside session JSON and no database shape change is proposed.
No new cross-layer server/client import boundary issue is evident from the spec; the dashboard table change stays within an existing client page.
No circular dependency or new shared mutable singleton risk is visible in the provided files.
